steps.heading: What is Gross Pay?
steps.body: Gross pay is the total amount you earn before anything is taken out. It's the number on your job offer — not what you actually receive. For salaried workers, gross pay is your annual salary divided by the number of paychecks per year.

steps.prompt: You negotiated a $52,000 salary. Your first paycheck shows $1,538. What happened to the rest?

feedback.correct: Exactly. Taxes and deductions are taken out before you ever see the money.
feedback.incorrect: The difference is taxes and deductions — they're removed before the money reaches you.

steps.hint: Think about what governments and employers take out of every paycheck automatically.

retry_variants.prompt: A friend earns $800/week but only receives $596. The missing $204 most likely went to:
options: A savings account they set up

retry_variants.prompt: Someone accepts a job paying $3,000/month but their first deposit is $2,190. What explains the gap?
options: Direct deposit takes 2–3 days to clear
Taxes and deductions reduced the amount
They were paid for only part of the month

steps.prompt: Marcus works 40 hours at $18/hr this week. His paycheck shows $518.40 deposited. What do we call the $720 he actually earned?
options: Gross pay — the total before deductions
Taxable income — what gets taxed only

feedback.correct: Right. Gross pay is the full amount earned before any taxes or deductions.
feedback.incorrect: The $720 (hours × rate) is gross pay. The $518.40 deposited is net pay — what's left after deductions.

steps.hint: Gross = what you earn. Net = what you keep.

retry_variants.prompt: A job offer says $48,000/year. After taxes, your actual take-home is about $34,000. Which figure is your gross pay?
After deductions, Jana receives $1,100 per paycheck. She earned $1,450 before anything was taken out. What is her gross pay?

steps.prompt: Maya earns $800 this week. Deductions total $160. What is her net pay?

feedback.correct: Correct! $800 − $160 = $640 take-home.
feedback.incorrect: Subtract deductions from gross: $800 − $160 = $640.

steps.hint: Net pay = Gross pay − Total deductions.

retry_variants.prompt: Jordan earns $950 this week. Deductions total $190. What is Jordan's net pay?
Sam's gross pay is $1,200. After $288 in deductions, what does Sam take home?

steps.heading: How Taxes Reduce Your Pay
steps.body: The government automatically withholds income tax from every paycheck. Most workers pay federal income tax plus state income tax — though some states have none. Federal tax is progressive: higher earners pay a higher percentage, but not on every dollar equally.

steps.prompt: Which of these is a mandatory paycheck deduction?

feedback.correct: Right. Federal income tax is withheld automatically — no opt-out.
feedback.incorrect: Bonuses and commissions are additions to your pay. Federal income tax is mandatory.

steps.hint: Mandatory means the government requires it — you have no choice.

retry_variants.prompt: Which deduction do ALL U.S. employees have automatically taken from their paycheck?
Your coworker says one of these is optional. Which one is actually required by law?
options: State income tax (in states that have it)

steps.prompt: Sam earns $32,000/year and pays 12% federal tax. Priya earns $180,000 and pays 24% on her higher income. What describes the U.S. federal income tax system?
options: Flat tax — everyone pays the same rate
Progressive — higher earners pay a higher percentage
Regressive — lower earners pay a higher percentage

feedback.correct: Correct. Progressive means the rate rises with income — Sam pays 12%, Priya pays 24%.
feedback.incorrect: This is progressive taxation: the percentage paid increases as income increases.

steps.hint: Think about what 'progressive' means — the rate scales upward with earnings.

retry_variants.prompt: Someone's income jumps from $40,000 to $90,000. Under progressive taxation, what happens to their federal tax rate?
options: It stays the same — rates never change mid-year
It increases as they enter higher tax brackets
It decreases because they contribute more to the economy
It only changes if they itemize deductions

retry_variants.prompt: Which person likely pays the highest percentage of their income in federal taxes?
options: All three pay the same rate

steps.prompt: Kim just moved from Texas (no state income tax) to California (state income tax) for a new job. What change should she expect on her paycheck?
options: Her federal tax rate will increase
A state income tax line will appear as a new deduction
Her gross pay will automatically decrease

feedback.correct: Correct. California withholds state income tax — a deduction that didn't exist when Kim was in Texas.
feedback.incorrect: Moving to a state with income tax adds a new deduction line. Texas has none; California does.

steps.hint: Some states don't have income tax at all — others do.

retry_variants.prompt: Alex lives in Florida (no state income tax). His friend in Oregon has a state tax line on his stub. What explains the difference?
options: Alex has higher federal deductions that replace state tax
Each state decides whether to collect state income tax
Federal tax covers state expenses in Florida
FICA is higher in Oregon to compensate

retry_variants.prompt: A paycheck from Washington State (no income tax) and one from New York will differ because:
options: New York withholds state income tax; Washington does not
Federal tax is higher in high-cost states
Employers in New York pay more into Medicare

steps.heading: FICA: Social Security & Medicare
steps.body: FICA is a separate mandatory deduction that funds two programs: Social Security (6.2% of your gross pay) and Medicare (1.45%). Combined, FICA is always 7.65% — taken from every paycheck with no exceptions.

steps.prompt: FICA deductions fund which two federal programs?

feedback.correct: Correct. Social Security covers retirement and disability; Medicare covers healthcare for seniors.
feedback.incorrect: FICA funds Social Security and Medicare — not Medicaid or unemployment.

steps.hint: One program funds retirement, the other funds healthcare for people 65+.

retry_variants.prompt: You see two FICA lines on your stub: one for retirement benefits, one for senior healthcare. What are they?
FICA supports Americans who are older or disabled. Which two programs?

steps.prompt: Social Security takes 6.2% of gross pay. You earn $500. How much is deducted?

retry_variants.prompt: Social Security takes 6.2%. You earn $800. How much is your Social Security deduction?
Your gross paycheck is $1,000. Social Security is 6.2%. How much goes to Social Security?

steps.prompt: FICA takes 7.65% of your gross pay — 6.2% for Social Security plus 1.45% for Medicare. Your paycheck shows $800 gross. How much is deducted for FICA in total?

feedback.correct: Right! $800 × 0.0765 = $61.20 — that's Social Security ($49.60) + Medicare ($11.60) combined.
feedback.incorrect: Multiply gross pay by the combined rate: $800 × 0.0765 = $61.20.

steps.hint: FICA = Social Security (6.2%) + Medicare (1.45%) = 7.65% total. Multiply gross by 0.0765.

retry_variants.prompt: Your gross pay is $600. FICA is 7.65% combined. What is your total FICA deduction?
Your pay stub shows: Social Security deducted $74.40, Medicare deducted $17.40. What is your total FICA deduction?

steps.heading: Net Pay & Budgeting
steps.body: Net pay is what actually hits your bank account after all taxes and deductions are removed. This is the number you should budget from — not your salary. Pre-tax deductions like a 401(k) or health insurance reduce your taxable income, which lowers what you owe in taxes.

steps.prompt: You earn $1,000 gross. You contribute $100 to a pre-tax 401(k). Assuming a 20% income tax rate, how much income tax do you owe?

feedback.correct: Exactly. The $100 401(k) is deducted first, so taxable income is $900. Tax = 20% × $900 = $180 — you save $20 vs. no contribution.
feedback.incorrect: Pre-tax means the 401(k) reduces taxable income. $1,000 − $100 = $900 taxable. 20% × $900 = $180.

steps.hint: Pre-tax deductions come out before income tax is calculated.

retry_variants.prompt: Alex's gross pay is $1,200. He contributes $150 to a pre-tax health plan. What is his taxable income?
Without any pre-tax deductions, you'd owe $240 in income taxes (24% of $1,000). You add a $200 pre-tax 401(k). How much do you now owe in income taxes?

steps.prompt: Alex earns $15/hr and works 40 hours. Total deductions are 28%. What is Alex's net pay?
steps.hint: Step 1: hours × rate = gross. Step 2: multiply by (1 − deduction rate).

retry_variants.prompt: Jamie earns $20/hr and works 35 hours. Deductions are 25%. What is Jamie's net pay?
Taylor earns $12/hr, works 40 hours, and has 30% in deductions. What is Taylor's net pay?

steps.prompt: You got a $45,000/year job offer. You're apartment hunting. Which number should you budget from?

feedback.correct: Smart. A $45K salary nets roughly $32–34K after taxes. Always budget from net.
feedback.incorrect: Budget from net pay, not gross. A $45K salary nets around $32–34K after taxes.

steps.hint: Rent and bills are paid from what you actually receive, not your contract number.

retry_variants.prompt: You earn $60,000/year. Which number is most useful for planning monthly expenses?
options: The number before your last raise

retry_variants.prompt: A friend says 'just divide your $50K salary by 12 for your monthly budget.' What's wrong?
options: That gives gross, not net — taxes haven't been subtracted

steps.prompt: Match each deduction to what it funds.

feedback.correct: Perfect. Each deduction funds a specific program or level of government.
feedback.incorrect: Review: Social Security → retirement; Medicare → senior healthcare; Federal → public services; State → local programs.

steps.hint: Think: retirees, senior healthcare, national services, local services.

retry_variants.prompt: Which deduction funds retirement income for Americans when they stop working?
Which deduction pays for healthcare for Americans who are 65 or older?

steps.heading: Reading a Real Pay Stub
steps.body: A pay stub lists your gross pay at the top, then each deduction line by line (federal tax, state tax, Social Security, Medicare, and any benefit elections). Subtract all deductions from gross to find your net pay. Every line has a specific purpose.

steps.prompt: Read Jordan's pay stub. What is Jordan's net pay?
steps.hint: Add up all deductions first, then subtract the total from gross pay.

retry_variants.prompt: Sam's gross pay is $900. Deductions: Federal $63, State $31.50, Social Security $55.80, Medicare $13.05. What is Sam's net pay?
Gross pay: $1,500. Deductions: $105 federal, $52.50 state, $93 SS, $21.75 Medicare. What is net pay?

final_evaluation.question: You earn $1,000 gross. Federal tax: 10%, state tax: 4%, FICA: 7.65%. What is your net pay?

retry_variants.prompt: You earn $800 gross. Federal tax: 10%, state tax: 4%, FICA: 7.65%. What is your net pay?

final_evaluation.question: Rashida just retired. She receives a monthly Social Security check and uses Medicare for her doctor visits. Which payroll deduction she paid throughout her career funded both of these benefits?

feedback.correct: Correct. FICA — the 7.65% deduction split between Social Security and Medicare — is what funded both benefits Rashida now uses.
feedback.incorrect: FICA (7.65% of every paycheck) funded both: Social Security provides her retirement income, Medicare covers her healthcare.

retry_variants.prompt: A coworker says 'FICA and federal income tax are basically the same — both are mandatory government deductions.' What's the key difference?
options: They are the same — both go to the general government budget
FICA funds specific programs (Social Security and Medicare); income tax goes to general government spending
Federal income tax is optional with the right W-4 form; FICA is not
FICA only applies to salaried workers, not hourly workers

final_evaluation.question: Your gross pay is $1,000. You contribute $150 to a pre-tax 401(k). With a 20% income tax rate, how much income tax do you owe?

feedback.correct: $150 comes out before taxes, so taxable income = $1,000 − $150 = $850. Tax = 20% × $850 = $170.
feedback.incorrect: Pre-tax means the 401(k) reduces taxable income first. $1,000 − $150 = $850 taxable. 20% × $850 = $170.

retry_variants.prompt: Gross pay: $1,500. Pre-tax health plan: $200. Tax rate: 25%. What is the taxable income after the pre-tax deduction?

final_evaluation.question: Maria earns $800/week and takes home $576. What percentage is being deducted?

feedback.correct: Right! $224 deducted ÷ $800 = 28%.

retry_variants.prompt: Chris earns $500/week and takes home $375. What percentage is being deducted?

final_evaluation.question: Pay stub: Gross $600 | Federal Tax −$42 | State Tax −$21 | Social Security −$37.20 | Medicare −$8.70. What is the net pay?

retry_variants.prompt: Gross $750 | Federal Tax −$52.50 | State Tax −$26.25 | Social Security −$46.50 | Medicare −$10.88. What is net pay?

final_evaluation.question: You got a job paying $42,000/year. You estimate your take-home will be about 72% of gross. Roughly how much will you have to budget each month?

feedback.correct: $42,000 × 0.72 = $30,240 net/year. $30,240 ÷ 12 = $2,520/month. Always budget from net — not gross.
feedback.incorrect: Net annual = $42,000 × 72% = $30,240. Monthly budget = $30,240 ÷ 12 = $2,520. Never budget from gross salary.

retry_variants.prompt: Someone earns $36,000/year and takes home 75% after deductions. What is their estimated monthly net pay?
